title: Hungary Removes Orbán-Era President, Ten Nations Launch Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition in Paris, Volkswagen Plans 100,000 Job Cuts
subhead: Hungary's new government ousts Viktor Orbán's handpicked president and constitutional court chief in a sweeping institutional reset. Ukraine and nine European countries form an integrated missile defence coalition as Russia pounds Kyiv. Volkswagen doubles its job-cut target to 100,000 as Chinese competition erodes profits.

sections.heading: Hungary Dismantles Orbán's Institutional Legacy
sections.body:
Hungary's parliament voted on Monday to remove President Tamás Sulyok from office and dismiss Constitutional Court chief Péter Polt, in the most dramatic move yet by Prime Minister Péter Magyar's new government since its landslide victory over Orbán's Fidesz party in April.

The vote, carried by the Tisza party's two-thirds majority through the 17th constitutional amendment, also removes Constitutional Court judges over the age of 70 and bars legislators who have served three or more terms from running again — a provision that applies to more than half of Fidesz's current parliamentary caucus. Sulyok now has five days to sign the amendment — effectively his own removal — or refer it to the Constitutional Court. Magyar has threatened impeachment proceedings if he does.

The amendment dismantles key pillars of what critics call Orbán's 'winner takes all' state architecture, built during 16 years of Fidesz rule. Fidesz deputies walked out before the vote, accusing the Tisza party of building its own tyranny. András Baka, former head of Hungary's Supreme Court, told the BBC that the move was necessary to break up 'a sophisticated authoritarian regime designed to survive even after electoral defeat', though he criticised the term-limit provision for restricting voters' choices. Orbán himself has rarely been seen in public since the election and left Hungary on Monday to attend the World Cup finals in the US. Fidesz's parliamentary group leader Gergely Gulyás also resigned on Monday, deepening the party's internal crisis.
sections.whyItMatters: Hungary is executing the most aggressive institutional de-Orbánisation attempted in any EU state that experienced democratic backsliding. The scale and speed of the purge raises questions about whether Magyar is rebuilding checks and balances or replacing one dominant party's architecture with another.

sections.heading: Ten Nations Form Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition as Russia Strikes Kyiv
sections.body:
Ukraine and nine European countries — Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Norway, Spain, Sweden, and the UK — formally launched the Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition at a meeting of the 'Coalition of the Willing' in Paris on Monday. The group's first priority is developing the Freyja project: a European-backed, lower-cost alternative to the US Patriot system that Ukraine can deploy at scale.

The announcement came hours before Russia launched a new ballistic missile attack on Kyiv overnight, with mayor Vitali Klitschko reporting fires in the Holosiivskyi district. Ukraine has been largely unable to intercept ballistic missiles — which travel at several times the speed of sound — for the past month due to critical shortages of air defence munitions. Russia has intensified drone and missile barrages against civilian targets in recent weeks, killing at least nine people in cross-border attacks on Sunday.

French President Emmanuel Macron also announced that Ukraine has ordered 16 Rafale fighter jets and Franco-Italian air defence systems, and received a licence to produce French SCALP cruise missiles domestically. Macron said coalition members had agreed to begin military exercises in countries neighbouring Ukraine, as part of a plan to deploy a multinational force there once a ceasefire is reached.

Zelenskyy said the Freyja project would 'supplement, not replace, existing systems' and create 'a strong shield over the entirety of Europe'. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov dismissed the Paris summit as a 'coalition of warmongers'.
sections.whyItMatters: The Freyja project and the new coalition represent Europe's most concrete step toward an independent air defence capability that reduces reliance on US Patriot systems. If successful, it would give Ukraine a scalable interceptor and reshape Europe's long-term defence industrial base.

sections.heading: Trump Claims Hormuz Control and Imposes 20% Cargo Toll as UAE Reports Iranian Attack on Tankers
sections.body:
President Donald Trump announced on Monday that the US will impose a 20% charge on all cargo shipped through the Strait of Hormuz and reinstate a naval blockade of Iranian ports, declaring the US 'THE GUARDIAN OF THE HORMUZ STRAIT'. The blockade takes effect at 20:00 GMT on Tuesday. The International Maritime Organization rejected the concept, stating there is 'no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait'.

The announcement came as the UAE reported that Iranian cruise missiles struck two of its national tankers in Omani waters within the strait, killing one Indian crew member and wounding eight others — four seriously. Iran's IRGC said it targeted 'several violating vessels' in the strait and shot down a US-made drone near Bandar Abbas. The Iranian army also claimed a drone attack on US military targets in Kuwait, while the IRGC said it struck weapons depots, a satellite communications centre, and a US forces building at al-Juffair Base in Bahrain.

US Central Command confirmed a third consecutive night of strikes on Iran, hitting targets in Bushehr, Chabahar, Jask, Konarak, Abu Musa, and Bandar Abbas over five hours. Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi pushed back on Trump's Guardian claim, posting: 'Iran has always been the GUARDIAN of the Strait and will remain so FOREVER. 20% is of course too much. We will be fair.' Ship-tracking firm Kpler reported strait crossings fell 52% between July 10-12 compared to the previous week. Oil prices rose more than 9%, with Brent crude hitting approximately $81 a barrel, its highest since mid-June.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's 20% toll and renewed blockade represent a sharp escalation that international maritime law explicitly does not support, creating legal and diplomatic friction with allies and shipping nations. The UAE tanker attack marks the first confirmed Iranian strike killing a crew member of a non-US vessel, raising the risk of broader Gulf state entanglement.

sections.heading: Volkswagen Plans Up to 100,000 Job Cuts as Chinese Competition Bites
sections.body:
Volkswagen Group CEO Oliver Blume confirmed that the company is preparing to cut up to 100,000 jobs globally — double the 50,000 previously announced. In a memo to staff, Blume said VW's costs are 20% higher than rival businesses and that the group 'must reduce our costs'. The company has been unable to confirm alternative uses for four German factories — in Zwickau, Emden, Hanover, and Neckarsulm — that were previously threatened with closure.

VW's operating profit fell from €22.6bn in 2023 to €19.1bn in 2024 and just €8.9bn last year. Sales in China, once one of its most lucrative markets, dropped 26% in the first half of 2026. US sales fell more than 7%, partly due to Trump-era tariffs on car imports. Chinese manufacturers have been moving aggressively into international markets with new technologies and lower production costs, slashing profit margins for established European brands.

Some industry analysts told Agence France-Presse that the 100,000 figure may be a negotiating tactic, with the final number likely lower. Mass protests took place at VW sites across Germany last week ahead of the supervisory board meeting. The company previously agreed with union IG Metall to cut 35,000 jobs at the VW brand by 2030, plus 15,000 at other brands, in what it called a 'socially responsible manner'.
sections.whyItMatters: VW's crisis is the most visible symptom of Europe's competitive failure against Chinese automakers. If 100,000 cuts materialise, it would be one of the largest industrial layoffs in European history and would amplify political pressure on the EU to tighten trade defences against China.

sections.heading: Fontainebleau Fire Investigated as Arson as Europe's Heatwave Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
A wildfire described by officials as 'virulent' and of 'exceptional scale' has scorched 800 hectares of the Fontainebleau forest, about 40 miles south-east of Paris. France's interior minister Laurent Nunez said investigators found approximately 10 fire ignition points within a 1,000-metre perimeter, suggesting the blaze may have been deliberately set. Two people have been arrested in connection with the Fontainebleau fire, and 59 across the country for fires overall.

For the first time, firefighting planes were deployed from southern France to tackle fires in the Paris region. The blaze has partially closed France's main north-south highway and disrupted high-speed train services, with delays of up to six hours at Paris's Gare de Lyon. Three nuclear power stations were temporarily shut down to avoid discharging warm cooling water into overheated rivers.

The fires come amid a devastating wildfire season across Europe. In Spain, at least 13 people were killed by a wildfire in Almeria — one of the country's deadliest ever — including three British nationals. A survivor, Malcolm Timbrell, described how his wife and friends attempted to flee on foot from a fire wall moving at over 20 kilometres per hour; he survived by sheltering in the last of six cars, four of which instantly combusted. In the UK, a wildfire in north Wales was declared a major incident. France's civil security director said wildfires have already covered 25,000 hectares in the country this year. Europe is the fastest-warming continent, heating at twice the global average according to the Copernicus climate service.
sections.whyItMatters: The suspected arson at Fontainebleau adds a security dimension to Europe's climate emergency. With 25,000 hectares already burned in France alone this year and heatwaves forcing nuclear plant shutdowns, the intersection of climate stress and deliberate fire-starting creates a compounding crisis that European infrastructure is not built to handle.

sections.heading: EU Sanctions Russian Cyber Operatives as Digital Front Escalates
sections.body:
The European Union imposed sanctions on nine individuals and four entities over what it called a years-long Russian cyber-espionage campaign targeting at least nine member states, including France, Germany, Poland, and the Netherlands. The European Council said those targeted 'contribute to Russia's efforts to destabilise the EU, its member states and international partners'.

Germany's Foreign Office summoned the Russian ambassador over what it called 'unacceptable' cyber attacks, saying they would be met decisively. The sanctions come as the EU's digital conflict with Russia intensifies alongside the kinetic war in Ukraine, with cyber operations targeting critical infrastructure, government systems, and democratic processes across the bloc.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is systematically building a sanctions framework for cyber operations, treating digital espionage as a national security threat on par with conventional aggression. Germany's decision to summon the Russian ambassador signals that cyber attacks are now triggering diplomatic consequences at the same level as physical incursions.

bottomLine: Hungary's institutional purge of Orbán-era appointees and the formation of a ten-nation European missile defence coalition mark the day's two biggest strategic shifts: one country dismantling an authoritarian legacy from within, and a continent building independent military capacity it has long outsourced to the US. Meanwhile, Trump's 20% Hormuz toll and the UAE tanker attack push the Gulf crisis into a more legally contested and commercially disruptive phase, VW's potential 100,000-job cut signals Europe's industrial vulnerability to Chinese competition, and suspected arson at Fontainebleau shows that Europe's climate emergency now has a security dimension.
